content:
# Leviticus Chapter 6 — The EMET Bible (Literal)

**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 60r–60v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/LEV_006_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_006_page_002.jpg`; **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 90–91 (LXX) | `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/LEV_006_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_006_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

*[Note on versification: MT Lev 6:1-23 = English Lev 6:8-30. The MT numbers chapter 6 beginning at what English Bibles call 5:20; what English Bibles call Lev 6:1-7 is MT Lev 5:20-26, already translated in LEV_005. This chapter (English 6:8-30) is MT 6:1-23 in the Hebrew canon. English verse numbers are used throughout below; MT equivalents are noted parenthetically at section headings.]*

---

**8** [MT 6:1] And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying:

**9** [MT 6:2] "Command [tsav] Aaron and his sons, saying: This is the torah [instruction] of the burnt offering [zot torat ha-olah] — it is the burnt offering [hi ha-olah] upon the hearth [al moqedah] on the altar [al ha-mizbeach] all night [kol ha-lailah] until morning, and the fire of the altar [esh ha-mizbeach] shall be kept burning on it [tuqad bo].

**10** [MT 6:3] And the priest shall put on his linen garment [mido vad] and linen breeches [miknese-vad] he shall put on against his flesh [al-besaro]; and he shall lift up [ve-herim] the ashes [ha-deshen] that the fire has consumed [tokhal ha-esh] with the burnt offering on the altar, and he shall place them [ve-samo] beside the altar [etsel ha-mizbeach].

**11** [MT 6:4] Then he shall strip off [u-fashat] his garments and put on [ve-lavash] other garments [begadim acherim], and he shall carry forth [ve-hotsi] the ashes outside [el-mihutz] the camp to a clean place [el-maqom tahor].

**12** [MT 6:5] And the fire on the altar shall be kept burning on it [tuqad vo]; it shall not go out [lo tikhbeh]; and the priest shall burn wood upon it [u-vi'er aleyha ets] morning by morning [ba-boqer ba-boqer]; and he shall arrange the burnt offering upon it [ve-arakha aleha ha-olah] and burn the fat [ve-hiqtir aleha chelvei] of the peace offerings [ha-shelamim] upon it.

**13** [MT 6:6] A perpetual fire [esh tamid] shall be kept burning on the altar [tuqad al-ha-mizbeach]; it shall not go out [lo tikhbeh].

**14** [MT 6:7] "And this is the torah [instruction] of the grain offering [zot torat ha-minchah]: the sons of Aaron shall bring it near [yaqrivu otah] before the LORD, in front of the altar [lifney ha-mizbeach].

**15** [MT 6:8] And he shall lift up from it [ve-herim mimmennu] with his handful [be-qumtso] from the fine flour of the grain offering [mi-solet ha-minchah] and from its oil [mi-shamnah], and all the frankincense [ve-et kol-ha-levonah] that is upon the grain offering, and he shall burn it on the altar as a pleasing aroma [le-nichoach] — its representative portion [azkaratah] — to the LORD.

**16** [MT 6:9] And the remainder of it [ve-ha-noteret mimmenah] Aaron and his sons shall eat [yokhelu]; unleavened it shall be eaten [matsot te'akhel] in the court of the tent of meeting [be-chatzar ohel mo'ed]; they shall eat it [yokheluha].

**17** [MT 6:10] It shall not be baked leavened [lo te'afeh chametz]; their portion I have given it [chelqam natatti otah] from my fire offerings [me-ishi]; it is most holy [qodesh qodashim], like the sin offering [ke-ha-chattat] and like the guilt offering [ke-ha-asham].

**18** [MT 6:11] Every male among the sons of Aaron [kol-zakhar mi-bney aharon] shall eat it — a perpetual due [chuqat olam] throughout your generations [le-doroteikhem] from the fire offerings of the LORD; everything that touches them [kol asher-yigga bahem] shall become holy [yiqdash].

**19** [MT 6:12] And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying:

**20** [MT 6:13] "This is the offering of Aaron and his sons [zot qorban aharon u-vanav] that they shall offer to the LORD on the day of his anointing [be-yom himashach oto]: one-tenth of an ephah [asaron solet] of fine flour as a continual grain offering [minchah tamid], half of it in the morning [machatzitah ba-boqer] and half of it in the evening [u-machatzitah ba-erev].

**21** [MT 6:14] On a griddle [al machabat] it shall be made with oil [be-shemen ye'aseh]; soaked [murbekheth] you shall bring it — a grain offering of broken pieces [tufini minchah] you shall offer as a pleasing aroma to the LORD.

**22** [MT 6:15] And the anointed priest [ha-kohen ha-mashiach] who is in his place from his sons [asher-yimashach tachatav mi-banav] shall do it — a perpetual statute [chuqat olam] to the LORD; it shall be wholly burned [kalil tuqtar].

**23** [MT 6:16] And every grain offering of the priest [kol-minchah le-kohen] shall be wholly [kalil] — it shall not be eaten.

**24** [MT 6:17] And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying:

**25** [MT 6:18] "Speak to Aaron and to his sons, saying: This is the torah [instruction] of the sin offering [zot torat ha-chattat]: in the place where the burnt offering is slaughtered [bimqom asher tisshachet ha-olah] the sin offering shall be slaughtered before the LORD [tisshachet ha-chattat lifney YHWH] — it is most holy [qodesh qodashim].

**26** [MT 6:19] The priest who offers it as a sin offering [ha-kohen asher yechattena] shall eat it [yokhalenah]; in the court of the tent of meeting [be-chatzar ohel mo'ed] it shall be eaten [te'akhel].

**27** [MT 6:20] Everything that touches its flesh [kol asher-yigga bi-vsarah] shall become holy [yiqdash]; and when any of its blood is splashed [asher yizzeh mi-damo] upon a garment [al-ha-beged], that which was splashed [asher yizzeh alav] you shall wash [tekhavves] in a holy place [bi-maqom qadosh].

**28** [MT 6:21] And the earthen vessel [u-kli-cheres] in which it was boiled [asher tevushal-bo] shall be broken [yishaver]; and if it was boiled in a bronze vessel [u-vi-kli nechoshet] it shall be scoured [ve-moraq] and rinsed with water [ve-shutaf ba-mayim].

**29** [MT 6:22] Every male among the priests [kol-zakhar ba-kohanim] shall eat it — it is most holy [qodesh qodashim].

**30** [MT 6:23] But no sin offering [ve-khol-chattat] from whose blood any was brought [asher yuva mi-damo] into the tent of meeting to make atonement in the holy place [le-khapper ba-qodesh] shall be eaten; it shall be burned with fire [ba-esh tissaref].

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

### Manuscript Observations (Leningrad Codex B19A, Folios 60r–60v)

1. **v.9 [MT 6:2] — Zot torat ha-olah / "This is the torah/instruction of the burnt offering":** The formula זֹאת תּוֹרַת הָעֹלָה (zot torat ha-olah) marks a major structural hinge in Leviticus. The word תּוֹרָה (torah) here functions not as a reference to the Pentateuch as a whole but in its basic sense: "instruction, ruling, directive, procedural law." It is the priest's operational manual for a specific sacrificial category. Similar formulas introduce the grain offering (v.14/MT 6:7), the sin offering (v.25/MT 6:18), the guilt offering (Lev 7:1), the ordination offering (Lev 7:37), and eventually the full summary formula at Lev 7:37-38 (zot ha-torah le-olah la-minchah ve-la-chattat ve-la-asham...). The chapter 6-7 complex is thus a Torah-within-Torah: a priestly manual within the broader Torah. The addressee in this section has shifted: chapters 1-5 were addressed to the people and individual Israelites; now with tsav ("command") in v.9 the address is specifically to Aaron and his sons — the priests. This is instruction for officiants, not offerers. The LXX renders Αὕτη ὁ νόμος τῆς ὁλοκαυτώσεως (Hautē ho nomos tēs holokautōseōs, "This is the law of the burnt offering"), using νόμος (nomos, "law/ordinance") for torah and ὁλοκαύτωσις (holokautōsis, "whole-burnt offering") for olah. The LXX term holokautōsis — "completely burned thing" — captures the defining feature of the olah: it is entirely consumed on the altar, unlike other offerings where parts are distributed to priests or offerers.

2. **v.9 [MT 6:2] — Al moqedah / "upon the hearth":** מוֹקְדָה (moqedah, "its burning place / the hearth of it") is a hapax legomenon — it appears only here in the Hebrew Bible. The root y-q-d means "to burn / to kindle"; moqedah is the noun designating the burning-place or fire-place of the altar. The suffix -ah is a third-person feminine possessive referring back to ha-olah (the burnt offering, feminine) — "upon its burning-place." The image is precise: the olah lies on the place of fire on the altar's grate throughout the night. The perpetual burning throughout the night is significant: it ensures that the altar fire never goes completely cold and that the morning burns find still-glowing coals. The LXX renders ἐπὶ τὸ αὐτὸ (epi to auto, "upon the same / in the same place"), which is a free rendering — the LXX does not attempt to render moqedah directly but conveys the locative sense.

3. **vv.10-11 [MT 6:3-4] — The ash-removal ritual (terumah ha-deshen / hotsasat ha-deshen):** The priest's handling of the deshen (ashes, or more precisely "fatty ash" — from dashan, "to be fat") is a two-stage process: (1) donning linen vestments and lifting the ashes beside the altar (v.10); (2) changing to other garments and carrying the ashes outside the camp (v.11). Stage 1 uses the verb הֵרִים (herim, "to lift up"), which is the same verb used for the priestly contribution offering (terumah). The ashes are not simply scooped away — they are formally "lifted" and set beside the altar, a gesture of presentation or acknowledgment, before being removed. Stage 2 involves a change of garments (fashat... ve-lavash begadim acherim), which protects the sacred vestments from the lesser holiness of the outer camp while carrying the ash residue. The distinction of garment-grades (holy garments within the sanctuary; other garments for contact with the profane outside) reflects the graduated holiness of space in the Levitical worldview: tabernacle court = holy; outside the camp = profane. The ashes must be deposited at a מָקוֹם טָהוֹר (maqom tahor, "clean place") — pure even outside the camp, because the ash is the residue of what was once wholly consecrated to the LORD. The LXX renders ἐξοίσει αὐτό (exoisei auto, "he shall carry it out") and εἰς τόπον καθαρόν (eis topon katharon, "to a clean/pure place"), precise equivalents.

4. **vv.12-13 [MT 6:5-6] — Esh tamid / "perpetual fire" — doubled emphasis:** The command that the fire shall not go out (לֹא תִכְבֶּה, lo tikhbeh) appears twice in close succession — once at the end of v.12 in connection with the morning wood-arrangement ritual (ba-boqer ba-boqer, "morning by morning"), and then as a standalone declaratory sentence in v.13: "A perpetual fire [esh tamid] shall be kept burning on the altar; it shall not go out." The repetition is deliberate and structurally significant. The first occurrence (v.12) embeds the prohibition within the practical instruction (add wood, arrange the olah, burn the fat of shelamim); the second (v.13) states the principle in absolute, unconditional form. This literary doubling (instruction + principle) is characteristic of the priestly legislative style: the procedural command is followed by a summary formulation that elevates it to the level of standing ordinance. The phrase אֵשׁ תָּמִיד (esh tamid) — "perpetual fire / continual fire" — is theologically charged: the tamid ("continual / regular / perpetual") root governs the entire sacrificial calendar in the priestly system. The tamid burnt offering (Num 28:3-8) — the daily morning and evening olah — and the tamid bread of the Presence (Lev 24:8) are similarly permanent, uninterrupted institutions. The perpetual fire is the altar's continuous witness: the worship of Israel does not end at day's close nor sleep at night. The LXX renders πῦρ καόμενον ἀεί (pur kaomenon aei, "fire burning always / perpetually") and οὐ σβεσθήσεται (ou sbeisthēsetai, "it shall not be extinguished"), the verb sbenunnmi being the standard Greek for "to quench fire."

5. **v.15 [MT 6:8] — Ve-herim be-qumtso / "he shall lift up with his handful":** The קֹמֶץ (qomets, "handful / fistful") is the defining priestly gesture of the grain offering — the priest takes a full-fisted handful of the fine flour, oil, and all the frankincense and burns it as the azkarah ("representative/memorial portion") on the altar. This action was introduced at Lev 2:2; here it is restated as part of the officiants' instruction (torat ha-minchah). The lifting gesture (herim, hiphil of rum, "to raise up") frames the action as an elevation — even the taking of the handful is a formal presentation before the LORD before the burning. What the priest lifts and burns represents the whole grain offering; the remainder that he and his sons eat (v.16) is explicitly connected to the burned portion by the structure: the fire transforms the azkarah into ascending aroma, while the eating by the priests effects the holy consumption of the remainder. The LXX renders δράξεται (draxetai, "he shall grasp / take a handful"), from drassomai, "to seize with the hand," a word that conveys the firm, intentional grip involved.

6. **v.16 [MT 6:9] — Matsot te'akhel / "unleavened it shall be eaten":** The unleavened requirement for the grain offering remainder (and by extension the sin and guilt offering portions eaten by priests) is foundational to the priestly eating rules. חָמֵץ (chametz, leavened/fermented) is excluded from all grain offerings and priestly portions eaten in the sanctuary context (cf. Lev 2:11: "no grain offering that you bring to the LORD shall be made with leaven"). The court of the tent of meeting (chatzar ohel mo'ed) is the designated space for priestly eating of most-holy things — a liminal zone that is both part of the sanctuary complex and accessible to officiating priests. The spatial restriction on eating (within the court) serves the holiness gradients: the most holy things must be consumed in the most holy space; bringing priestly portions outside the court would profane them. The LXX renders ἄζυμα βρωθήσεται (azuma brōthēsetai, "it shall be eaten unleavened"), ἄζυμος (azymos, "without leaven") being the standard LXX term for matsot throughout the Pentateuch.

7. **v.17 [MT 6:10] — Qodesh qodashim / "most holy" — ke-chattat ve-ke-asham:** The declaration קֹדֶשׁ קָדָשִׁים הִוא (qodesh qodashim hi, "it is most holy") applies here to the priestly remainder of the grain offering and is immediately elaborated by comparison: "like the sin offering and like the guilt offering." This comparison achieves several things simultaneously: (a) it confirms that the grain offering remainder belongs to the "most holy" [qodesh qodashim] category; (b) it aligns the grain offering with the sin offering and guilt offering as a unified class of priestly-eaten most-holy offerings; (c) it establishes consistency of treatment across offering types. The contrast between the "most holy" offerings (chattat, asham, minchah, and certain shelamim portions: Lev 21:22) and the "holy" offerings (shelamim, firstfruits, tithes) governs who may eat where: most-holy things are eaten by male priests only, in the court; holy things may be eaten by the whole priestly household, in any ritually clean place within Jerusalem (or the camp in the wilderness setting). The LXX renders ἅγια τῶν ἁγίων ἐστίν (hagia tōn hagiōn estin, "it is holy of holies / most holy"), a literal rendering of the Hebrew superlative construction (construct chain: "holiness of holinesses").

8. **v.18 [MT 6:11] — Kol-zakhar mi-bney aharon yokhal otah / "every male among the sons of Aaron shall eat it":** The gender restriction (zakhar, "male") governing the priestly eating of most-holy things is stated here for the grain offering and is paralleled at v.29 [MT 6:22] for the sin offering. The restriction to male priests eating most-holy things in the court (as distinct from the broader permission for the priestly household to eat holy things in Lev 10:14, 22:13) reflects the graduated access structure of the sanctuary: the innermost holy actions — including consumption of most-holy portions — are restricted to those who actually officiate at the altar. The phrase חָק-עוֹלָם לְדֹרֹתֵיכֶם (chuq olam le-doroteikhem, "a perpetual due throughout your generations") recurs throughout the priestly legislation as the formulaic designation of a permanent institution — not time-limited, not conditional on circumstance, but an enduring right and obligation built into the structure of Israel's worship for all generations. The final clause — כֹּל אֲשֶׁר-יִגַּע בָּהֶם יִקְדָּשׁ (kol asher-yigga bahem yiqdash, "everything that touches them shall become holy") — expresses the contagious holiness of most-holy things: contact with the most-holy conveys holy status to whatever touches it. This "positive contagion" of holiness (as opposed to the negative contagion of impurity) is a distinctive feature of the sanctuary's inner court. The LXX renders ἁγιασθήσεται (hagiasēthēsetai, "it shall be made holy / sanctified"), preserving the passive-contagion sense.

9. **vv.20-21 [MT 6:13-14] — The high priest's daily grain offering (torat ha-kohen ha-mashiach):** The daily grain offering of the high priest — an asaron (one-tenth ephah) of fine flour, half in the morning and half in the evening, made on a griddle with oil — is introduced here as a standing institution. The phrase בְּיוֹם הִמָּשַׁח אֹתוֹ (be-yom himashach oto, "on the day of his anointing") is pivotal: it establishes that this regular daily offering is inaugurated on the day the priest is anointed into office and continues daily for the duration of his service. The word מִנְחָה תָמִיד (minchah tamid, "continual grain offering") aligns this priestly offering with the tamid system — perpetual, daily, without interruption. The preparation method — מֻרְבֶּכֶת (murbekheth, "thoroughly soaked / moistened / stirred") on a מַחֲבַת (machabat, "griddle / flat pan") with oil — produces a soaked or mixed dough rather than a firm cake. The word murbekheth (from r-b-k, "to soak, to moisten thoroughly") appears only here and at Lev 7:12 in the Levitical offering contexts; it denotes a wet-fried or well-soaked preparation distinct from the dry baking of v.17 [MT 6:10]. The offering is brought in תֻּפִינֵי (tufini, "broken pieces / crumbled portions") — the priest brings it crumbled or broken, not as a whole cake. This detail (also a rare word) emphasizes the thoroughness of the preparation and presentation. The LXX renders πεφυραμένην (pephuramenen, "thoroughly kneaded/mixed") for murbekheth and σεμίδαλιν κεκλασμένην (semidelin keklasmenēn, "crushed/broken fine flour") for the tufini minchah, both precise functional renderings.

10. **v.22 [MT 6:15] — Kalil tuqtar / "it shall be wholly burned":** כָּלִיל (kalil, "complete / entire / whole") is the adverb designating a whole-burning sacrifice — one entirely consumed on the altar with no remainder for the priests. The high priest's own grain offering is kalil: he eats none of it. This is in striking contrast to the regular grain offering (vv.14-18), where the priests eat the remainder (v.16). The logic is clear: the high priest cannot benefit from an offering he himself is making on his own behalf — he has no one above him to share the priestly portion. The principle that "every grain offering of the priest shall be wholly [offered]" (v.23/MT 6:16) extends this to all priestly self-offerings: when a priest brings his own minchah, it is entirely burned, not eaten. The LXX renders ὁλόκαυτος ἀναβήσεται (holokautos anabēsetai, "it shall go up as a whole burnt offering"), using the whole-offering vocabulary of the olah — theologically appropriate since the priestly self-offering shares the olah's defining characteristic of total consumption.

11. **v.25 [MT 6:18] — Bimqom asher tisshachet ha-olah tisshachet ha-chattat / "in the place where the burnt offering is slaughtered, the sin offering shall be slaughtered":** The spatial equation of the slaughter-place of the olah and the chattat is architecturally precise. According to the later priestly tradition (cf. Ezek 40:38-43; Mishnah Zevachim 5:1-4), the north side of the altar is the designated slaughter area for both the olah and the chattat when they are most-holy offerings. The repetition of the place-specification accomplishes two things: (1) it confirms that the sin offering belongs to the same zone of maximal ritual focus as the burnt offering; (2) it prevents the inadvertent degradation of the chattat by performing it in a less holy area. The LXX renders ἐν τόπῳ οὗ σφαγιάζουσιν τὸ ὁλοκαύτωμα (en topō hou sfagiazousin to holokautōma, "in the place where they slaughter the whole burnt offering"), preserving both the spatial identity and the active verb of slaughter.

12. **vv.27-28 [MT 6:20-21] — Contagious holiness of the chattat blood and the earthen/bronze vessel:** The blood of the sin offering, when splashed (nizzah) on a garment, imparts holiness sufficient to require laundering in a holy place — not simply washing. The verb כִּבֵּס (kibbes, "to wash / to launder by treading") is used for deliberate, vigorous cleaning — not casual rinsing. The spatial qualifier bi-maqom qadosh ("in a holy place") means the garment cannot simply be taken home and washed in an ordinary courtyard: the holiness of the blood requires that the washing be done in a sanctified location. The vessel legislation (v.28/MT 6:21) operates on the same principle: the sin offering's intense holiness is absorbed by the vessel in which it was cooked. An earthen vessel cannot be fully purified of this absorbed holiness (pottery is porous), so it must be broken. A bronze vessel, being non-porous, can be scoured (moraq, "scrubbed") and rinsed (shutaf ba-mayim, "rinsed with water") and thus purified for reuse. This distinction between porous and non-porous vessels mirrors the uncleanness legislation of Lev 11:33-35, where earthen vessels contaminated by uncleanness must be broken for the same reason — they cannot be adequately purged. The LXX renders συντριβήσεται (suntribēsetai, "it shall be shattered / crushed") for yishaver and ἐκνιφήσεται ἐν ὕδατι (ekniphēsetai en hudati, "shall be rinsed out in water") for shutaf ba-mayim, both appropriate renderings.

13. **v.30 [MT 6:23] — The sin offering whose blood was brought into the tent of meeting shall not be eaten:** The closing principle of the chapter draws the critical distinction between two classes of sin offering: (a) those whose blood is used for external altar-manipulation (blood on the outer altar horns and base) — these are eaten by the priests in the court (vv.26/MT 6:19); (b) those whose blood is brought inside the tent of meeting to the inner altar or the Holy of Holies (the communal sin offerings of Lev 4:1-21 for the priest or the whole congregation) — these may not be eaten and must be burned entirely. The distinction is logical: when the blood of a chattat is carried into the sanctuary interior on behalf of the offerer, the offering has performed its atonement function at the highest level of access to the divine presence. Its flesh cannot simultaneously be consumed as priestly food — it must be wholly surrendered. This principle of non-eating for the inside-blood chattat is reiterated at Lev 10:16-20 in the incident of the goat not being eaten after Nadab and Abihu's deaths. The LXX renders ἐν πυρὶ κατακαυθήσεται (en puri katakauthēsetai, "it shall be completely burned in fire"), using the intensive compound kata- prefix to convey total combustion.

---

### Structural Notes

Leviticus 6 (MT 6:1-23; English 6:8-30) is the second panel of the priestly manual that began with chapters 1-5. Whereas chapters 1-5 addressed the laity bringing offerings, chapters 6-7 address the officiating priests. The chapter is organized around four "torah" (instruction) formulas, each introducing a section:

| Section | Formula | MT Verses | English Verses | Content |
|---------|---------|-----------|----------------|---------|
| I | Zot torat ha-olah | 6:1-6 | 6:8-13 | Burnt offering: perpetual fire, ash removal |
| II | Zot torat ha-minchah | 6:7-11 | 6:14-18 | Grain offering: priest's portion, most-holy status |
| III | (No formula; continuation) | 6:12-16 | 6:19-23 | High priest's daily grain offering: wholly burned |
| IV | Zot torat ha-chattat | 6:17-23 | 6:24-30 | Sin offering: place of slaughter, holiness rules |

**Section I — Torah of the Burnt Offering (vv.8-13/MT 6:1-6):**

The burnt offering's distinctive features in this passage are not the slaughter and offering process (already given in Lev 1) but the overnight burn, the morning ash ritual, and above all the perpetual fire (esh tamid, vv.12-13). The altar fire is:
- To be tended with morning wood (ba-boqer ba-boqer, "morning by morning")
- Used to burn the fat of the peace offerings alongside the daily olah
- Never permitted to go out (lo tikhbeh — stated twice for emphasis)

The twice-stated lo tikhbeh creates the chapter's most memorable theological declaration: the altar of the LORD is never cold. Israel's worship is unbroken, day and night, generation after generation.

**Section II — Torah of the Grain Offering (vv.14-18/MT 6:7-11):**

The grain offering legislation for the priests specifies:
- The azkarah (handful with oil and all frankincense) is burned as the LORD's portion
- The remainder is eaten unleavened in the court by Aaron and his sons
- It is most holy (qodesh qodashim), classified with the sin and guilt offerings
- Access is restricted to male priests (kol-zakhar)
- Contact with the offering consecrates whatever touches it (positive holiness contagion)

**Section III — High Priest's Daily Grain Offering (vv.19-23/MT 6:12-16):**

This subsection introduces a special daily offering unique to the high priest:
- Asaron of fine flour, half morning / half evening, daily
- Made with oil on a griddle, brought in broken pieces
- Kalil (wholly burned) — the high priest eats none of his own offering
- The principle is extended: every priestly grain offering is wholly burned (v.23)

**Section IV — Torah of the Sin Offering (vv.24-30/MT 6:17-23):**

The sin offering's distinctive rules for the priests:
- Same slaughter-place as the burnt offering (north of the altar)
- The officiating priest eats it in the court (male priests only)
- Blood splashed on garments must be laundered in a holy place
- Earthen vessels used for cooking must be broken; bronze vessels scoured and rinsed
- The most-holy status applies: kol-zakhar ba-kohanim yokhal (v.29)
- Exception: sin offerings whose blood entered the tent of meeting are not eaten but wholly burned (v.30)

The governing theological concept throughout the chapter is **qodesh qodashim** (most holy). The phrase appears at vv.17, 25, and 29, anchoring all three of the main offering categories addressed (grain, sin) and structuring the gradations of access, eating rights, and spatial restriction. The chapter teaches the priests that their privilege of eating most-holy things comes with absolute conditions: right place, right persons, right preparation, right handling of vessels and garments — the holiness of what they eat demands scrupulous attention to every detail of its management.

---

### Vaticanus LXX Comparison Summary (pp. 90–91)

The Vaticanus pages (pa_0090–pa_0091) present the LXX text of Leviticus 6 in the characteristic Codex Vaticanus format — three columns of Greek uncials, without breathings or accents, in the 4th-century hand. The chapter header on page 90 is visible as "CAP. XXXIII" (continuing from Lev 5's sectional numbering in the Vaticanus's own chapter enumeration). The LXX chapter and verse structure for Leviticus 6 aligns with the MT (though, as noted, the LXX and English versification begins the chapter at what MT calls 5:20/6:1).

Key LXX observations for Leviticus 6 (English 6:8-30):

- **νόμος (nomos) for torah:** The LXX's translation of תּוֹרָה as νόμος (nomos, "law / ordinance / regulation") throughout these "zot torat" formulas is significant. Greek νόμος carries a sense of civic law, established regulation, and normative custom — broader and more juridical than the Hebrew torah's range of "instruction / teaching / guidance." The LXX rendering thus places the priestly manuals within a framework of binding law rather than pedagogical guidance. This choice of vocabulary had enormous influence on how later Greek-speaking readers (including the writers of the NT and early church fathers) understood the relationship between Torah and νόμος.

- **ὁλοκαύτωμα / ὁλοκαύτωσις (holokautōma / holokautōsis) for olah:** The LXX renders עֹלָה (olah, "burnt offering") with the compound ὁλοκαύτωμα or ὁλοκαύτωσις throughout — literally "a thing entirely burned" (holos + kaiō). This is an interpretive rather than etymological translation: olah means "what goes up / ascending [sacrifice]," from the root עָלָה (alah, "to go up"), emphasizing the upward movement of the sacrifice (ascending smoke to heaven). The LXX's "whole-burnt-offering" terminology instead emphasizes the completeness of combustion — a different aspect of the same sacrifice. Both renderings are theologically apt but capture different dimensions of the olah's meaning.

- **πῦρ τὸ διὰ παντός (pur to dia pantos, "the fire that goes throughout / perpetual fire"):** The LXX rendering of אֵשׁ תָּמִיד (esh tamid, "perpetual fire") uses the phrase "the fire that [goes] throughout," employing the Greek idiom dia pantos ("throughout all time / continuously") rather than a word-for-word rendering. The Vaticanus text uses this phrase at v.13/MT 6:6, preserving the perpetual / unbroken character of the altar fire. The additional οὐ σβεσθήσεται (ou sbeisthēsetai, "it shall not be quenched") follows exactly, matching lo tikhbeh of the MT.

- **ἅγια τῶν ἁγίων (hagia tōn hagiōn) for qodesh qodashim:** The superlative "holies of the holies / most holy" is rendered literally as a Greek genitive of the superlative type ("holiness of holinesses" → "holiest of holies"). This construction, preserved consistently in Vaticanus throughout Leviticus and the sanctuary descriptions of Exodus, becomes the standard Greek designation for the innermost sanctuary compartment as well — τὰ ἅγια τῶν ἁγίων (ta hagia tōn hagiōn, "the Holy of Holies"). The Vaticanus preserves this phrase clearly in the ritual context of priestly eating rights, demonstrating that qodesh qodashim is a category label applicable both to sacred space (the innermost chamber) and to sacred substance (the most-holy offerings).

- **MT/LXX alignment for Leviticus 6:** As with Leviticus 5, the Vaticanus LXX for Leviticus 6 tracks the MT very closely. The major structural elements — the four-section layout, the perpetual fire emphasis, the priestly eating rules, the vessel legislation, and the distinction between inside-blood and outside-blood sin offerings — are all present and accurately rendered. There are no notable expansions or omissions in the Vaticanus text for this chapter. The LXX translation consistently prioritizes functional equivalence (meaning and logical structure) over formal equivalence (word-for-word correspondence), while still preserving the technical vocabulary of the priestly system.

— EMET
